title: Theoretical study of superconductivity in freestanding infinite-layer nickelate membranes under pressure: mitigation of excess correlation enhances $T_c$
authors: Mahiru Seki, Reo Kono, Naotaka Tanaka, Kensei Ushio, Daiki Nakaoka, Masayuki Ochi, Kazuhiko Kuroki, Hirofumi Sakakibara
abstract: We theoretically investigate a freestanding membrane of infinite-layer nickelate Nd$_{0.85}$Sr$_{0.15}$NiO$_2$ under pressure by constructing a seven-orbital effective model based on first-principles calculations.   By performing the fluctuation exchange (FLEX) approximation, we demonstrate that the seven-orbital model explains a monotonic increase in $T_c$ reported in a recent experiment. This enhancement of superconductivity is attributed to the mitigation of excessively strong electron correlations caused by exceptionally low valence of Ni atom. Furthermore, we examine the dynamical stability of the crystal structure under pressure through phonon calculation.
summary: This study theoretically demonstrates that applying pressure to infinite-layer nickelate membranes increases superconductivity by reducing the extreme electron correlations present at low Ni valence. By using a seven-orbital model, the authors show that the reduction in Ni-O bond distance enhances the bandwidth and mitigates the strength of the Coulomb interaction.
main_problem: Understanding the mechanism behind the enhancement of the superconducting transition temperature (Tc) in infinite-layer nickelate membranes under high pressure.
main_result: The increase in Tc is driven by the mitigation of excessively strong electron correlations (reduced U/t ratio) caused by the reduction of Ni-O bond distances under pressure.
method: First-principles calculations (Quantum ESPRESSO) combined with the fluctuation exchange (FLEX) approximation and the linearized Eliashberg equation.
model_or_system: A freestanding infinite-layer nickelate membrane of Nd0.85Sr0.15NiO2 under external pressure (up to 90 GPa), modeled using a seven-orbital effective Hamiltonian including Ni-3d and Nd-5d orbitals.
key_observables: Superconducting transition temperature (Tc), eigenvalue of the Eliashberg equation (lambda), lattice constants (a and c), and phonon dispersion.
important_parameters: Onsite Coulomb (U, U') and exchange (J, J') parameters, hopping integrals (t1, t2, t3), and the ratio of interaction to hopping (U/t).
main_assumptions: Use of the virtual crystal approximation (VCA) for Sr doping and the construction of a seven-orbital model via Wannierization.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the study's gist, lattice constants as a function of pressure, and phonon dispersion; Figure 2 shows the electronic band structure.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of Tc enhancement, describes the structural optimization and stability analysis, details the construction of the seven-orbital model and interaction parameters, presents the results of the FLEX calculation regarding superconductivity, and concludes with the physical mechanism.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic diagram showing the gist of this study, (b) lattice constant a, c as functions of external pressure P, and (c) phonon dispersion of Nd0.85Sr0.15NiO2 under pressure are presented. The panel (c) shows the case of 2 × 2 × 2 supercells with a 6 × 6 × 6 k-point mesh, where the strength of external pressure is indicated.

FIG. 2. Band structure with respect to the Fermi level (EF = 0) of Nd0.85Sr0.15NiO2 under pressure for the cases of (a) 0 GPa and (b) 90 GPa. Here, the weight of Wannier orbitals are represented by the thickness of color coded lines.

FIG. 3. (a) Orbital occupancy of Wannier orbitals and (b) hopping integral t among the near neighbor Wannier dx2−y2 orbital as functions of external pressure P, where the destina- tions in the hopping are indicated. In the inset, the roundness parameter r = (|t2|+|t3|)/|t1| of the Fermi surface (red circle) and ∆Es = E4s −E3dx2−y2 (blue triangle) are plotted.

FIG. 5. Tc’s are plotted against the eigenvalues of Eliashberg equation λ at a fixed temperature, T = 0.01 eV. Red squares and green diamonds indicate the same λ with that of Fig. 4(b). Blue downward triangles indicate the data of cuprates and Ruddlesden-Popper type nickelates, calculated in the same method as described in Ref. [112]. Yellow hatched region is a guide to the eyes.

FIG. 4. Onsite intraorbital interaction Ux2−y2 (red circle, panel (a)), the ratio Ux2−y2/|t1| (blue triangle), and λ of d- wave superconductivity (panel (b) and (c)) are plotted as a function of pressure P. In panel (b), the original case (red squares) and the hypothetical cases equating only the param- eter set (t1, t2, t3) (blue circle) and only (t1, t2, t3, U, U ′, J, J′) (black asterisks) with those of the pressurized model are shown. In panel (c), green diamonds indicate the results obtained by using the value of Ref. [41]. The open upward (downward) triangles indicate the Ux2−y2 = 3.0 and 2.5 eV cases (see text), respectively. The cross symbols indicate those of Ux2−y2 = 3.0 eV at n =...
